question: MigrainePredict processes users' heart-rate and sleep data to predict migraine attacks. Before any processing starts, each user gives explicit consent on a dedicated opt-in screen that names this exact purpose, is separate from the general terms of service, can be refused without losing unrelated features, and can be withdrawn at any time. Is this processing of health data permitted?

A hospital neurologist treating a MigrainePredict user accesses the user's migraine-prediction data because it is necessary for the user's medical diagnosis and treatment, and does so under their professional duty of confidentiality. Is this access permitted?

MigrainePredict's operational logs are fully anonymised at the moment they are written, so no log entry can be linked to any person by any means, and the logs are kept for the whole life of the system. Does this logging practice comply with both regulations?
A user reports that the date of birth MigrainePredict stores about them is wrong. The company corrects it two days after the request. Has the company met its duty?

A user requests erasure of their data. MigrainePredict erases everything except one pseudonymised dataset that is genuinely necessary for an ongoing scientific study, which runs under the safeguards EU law requires for research (Article 89(1)). Is the partial refusal permitted?

MigrainePredict discovers a personal data breach that is likely to result in a risk to its users, and notifies the supervisory authority 48 hours after becoming aware of it. Does the notification meet the deadline duty?

MigrainePredict's prediction system is designed with a clinician dashboard through which trained staff can effectively oversee its outputs, intervene, and stop it while it is in use. Does this satisfy the AI Act's human-oversight design duty?

MigrainePredict collects sensor data for the specified, explicit purpose of predicting migraine attacks, states this purpose at sign-up, and uses the data only for that purpose. Does this collection-and-use practice comply with the purpose rules?

MigrainePredict keeps one historical dataset beyond its normal retention period solely for statistical purposes, with the technical and organisational safeguards the GDPR requires for such storage in place. May it keep this data for the longer period?
MigrainePredict sells users' migraine-risk scores to an advertising network. Users have not consented to this, and no other exception to the ban on processing health data applies. Is this permitted?

Sensor data that MigrainePredict collected solely for migraine prediction is later reused to build advertising profiles - a purpose incompatible with the original one - with no new consent and no other basis for the change. Does this comply with the purpose rules?
A user asks MigrainePredict to correct an inaccurate weight value it stores about them. The company refuses, saying corrections are too costly. Is the refusal compliant?

MigrainePredict notifies the supervisory authority of a personal data breach three weeks after discovering it, gives no reasons for the delay, and faced no obstacle to notifying earlier. Does this comply with the notification duty?

A breach exposes users' identifiable health records and is likely to result in a high risk to those users, but MigrainePredict decides not to inform the affected users at all. Is that decision compliant?

MigrainePredict automatically suspends a user's access to an insurance-linked discount based solely on its automated predictions, with no human involvement, and the suspension produces legal effects for the user. Is subjecting the user to this decision permitted?
MigrainePredict's high-risk prediction system is built with no capability to automatically record events during its operation. Does the system meet the AI Act's logging requirement?
The provider of MigrainePredict has never established, implemented or documented any risk management system for it. Is the provider meeting the AI Act's risk-management duty?

MigrainePredict keeps all users' identifiable sensor data indefinitely after they close their accounts, for no stated purpose, and no archiving, research or statistical use applies. Is this retention compliant?

MigrainePredict's own assessment concludes that a planned new feature is likely to result in a high risk to users' rights and freedoms, but the company launches the feature without first carrying out a data protection impact assessment. Is the launch compliant?
Can MigrainePredict retain operational logs for 90 days?

A MigrainePredict user has asked for all their personal data to be deleted, but the company wants to keep the data because it is needed for a scientific research study. Must the company delete the data?
MigrainePredict plans to keep users' raw identifiable sensor data for five years. Is that retention period acceptable?
MigrainePredict processes users' health data relying on consent that users gave two years ago inside an old app version's general sign-up flow. Is the processing lawfully based on that consent?
MigrainePredict outsources storage of user data to a cloud provider. Nothing is stated about the provider's technical and organisational guarantees. Is using this processor compliant?
MigrainePredict, a company of 20 employees that regularly processes users' health data, wants to rely on the small-organisation exemption to skip keeping records of its processing activities. Can it?
Does MigrainePredict need planning permission to repaint the interior walls of its office?
Is MigrainePredict allowed to negotiate a lower rent for its office lease?
Must MigrainePredict register its trademark in every EU member state before selling the app?

MigrainePredict publishes fully anonymised, aggregate monthly statistics about migraine frequency that cannot be linked to any individual by any means. Do the GDPR's data-processing obligations govern this publication?

MigrainePredict's website includes a static FAQ page built from fixed hand-written rules - it is not a machine-based system operating with any autonomy. Must that FAQ page meet the AI Act's logging duty for high-risk AI systems?

Before MigrainePredict begins processing a user's heart-rate and sleep data to predict migraine attacks, the user must complete a dedicated opt-in screen that spells out this exact purpose, sits apart from the general terms of service, can be declined without giving up unrelated features, and can be revoked whenever the user likes. Is it lawful for MigrainePredict to process this health data?

Is MigrainePredict permitted to use its users' sleep and heart-rate measurements to forecast migraine attacks, given that every user first grants explicit consent on a stand-alone opt-in page that names that specific purpose, is separate from the terms of service, can be refused while keeping all unrelated features, and can be withdrawn at any time?

MigrainePredict strips all identifying information from its operational logs the moment each entry is written, so nobody can ever be linked to a log entry by any means, and it then keeps those logs for as long as the system exists. Is this practice in line with both regulations?

If every entry in MigrainePredict's operational logs is anonymised at the point of writing - making it impossible, by any means, to connect an entry to a person - and the logs are retained over the entire life of the system, does the logging practice comply with both regulations?

After a user asks MigrainePredict to erase their data, the company deletes everything apart from a single pseudonymised dataset that an ongoing scientific study genuinely cannot do without, and that study operates under the safeguards EU law requires for research (Article 89(1)). May the company hold back that one dataset?

MigrainePredict receives an erasure request and complies in full except for one dataset: it is pseudonymised, genuinely necessary to an ongoing scientific study, and handled under the research safeguards EU law demands. Is keeping that dataset permitted?

MigrainePredict passes its users' migraine-risk scores to an advertising network in return for payment. The users never consented to this, and none of the other exceptions to the ban on processing health data applies. Is the sale allowed?
Without any user consent - and with no other exception applying - MigrainePredict monetises its users' migraine-risk scores by selling them to an ad network. Does this comply?

Three weeks after discovering a personal data breach, MigrainePredict finally informs the supervisory authority. It offers no explanation for the delay, and nothing had prevented it from reporting sooner. Is that notification compliant?

MigrainePredict waited twenty-one days after finding a personal data breach before telling the supervisory authority, gave no reasons for taking that long, and had faced no obstacle to earlier reporting. Did it meet its notification duty?

When users close their MigrainePredict accounts, the company nevertheless holds on to all of their identifiable sensor data indefinitely, without any stated purpose, and none of it is used for archiving, research or statistics. Is that retention lawful?

MigrainePredict never deletes former users' sensor data: after account closure the data stays, fully identifiable, indefinitely, serving no stated purpose and no archiving, research or statistical use. Does this comply with the storage rules?
Would it be acceptable for MigrainePredict to hold on to its operational logs for 90 days?
May MigrainePredict store the logs its system produces for a period of 90 days?

One of MigrainePredict's users demands that every piece of their personal data be erased. The company would rather keep it, since the data is needed for a scientific research study. Is it obliged to delete the data?

MigrainePredict wants to hold on to a user's personal data because a scientific research study needs it, even though the user has asked for all of it to be deleted. Does the company have to comply with the deletion request?

Each month MigrainePredict releases aggregate migraine-frequency statistics that are fully anonymised and cannot, by any means, be traced back to any individual. Are these releases subject to the GDPR's data-processing obligations?

Do the GDPR's obligations on processing apply when MigrainePredict publishes monthly migraine-frequency figures that are aggregated and anonymised so thoroughly that linking them to any person is impossible?

MigrainePredict's site has a plain FAQ page assembled from fixed, hand-written rules - nothing about it is a machine-based system operating with any autonomy. Does the AI Act's logging duty for high-risk AI systems apply to that page?

Is the AI Act's high-risk logging requirement binding on MigrainePredict's static website FAQ, which is built entirely from hand-written fixed rules and is not a machine-based system with any degree of autonomy?
